questions.stem: According to current legislation, drinking water is allowed to contain up to:
questions.explanation: Legislation requires 0 enterococci/100 mL — indicators of faecal contamination.

questions.stem: Water is essential for the human body for all of the following, except:

options.B: Provides energy to muscles during initial effort

questions.explanation: Water has no calories/energy — it cannot fuel muscles.

questions.stem: Which is less polluting?
questions.explanation: Natural gas is the cleanest-burning fossil fuel.

questions.stem: Which energy is non-renewable?
questions.explanation: Nuclear uses finite uranium; the others are renewable.

questions.stem: Regarding HEALTH EDUCATION methods, which is true?

options.B: Approach depends on characteristics & education level of trainees
options.C: Small groups show the best results

questions.explanation: Tailor the approach to the audience's characteristics & education level.

questions.stem: Hospital epidemics must be investigated for all except:

options.C: Let it evolve until natural immunity

questions.explanation: You never let an outbreak run its course.

questions.stem: Parasitic protozoa (emerging pathogens) include:
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium parvum is the emerging protozoan.

questions.stem: Bottled water must contain zero cfu/mL of the following, except:
questions.explanation: Heterotrophic flora is allowed a limit; true indicators must be 0.

questions.stem: Waters free of microorganisms (intervention retains microbes during creation) are:
questions.explanation: Groundwater is soil-filtered as it forms.

questions.stem: Regarding BMI, which is true?
questions.explanation: BMI cut-offs are not valid at all ages → none hold.

questions.stem: Food contamination occurs by all the following, except:
questions.explanation: Ventilation is not a contamination route.

questions.stem: Regarding epidemic outbreaks, which is incorrect?

options.B: A limited, clearly defined number of susceptibles

questions.explanation: An outbreak needs a sufficiently LARGE susceptible pool, not a limited one.

questions.stem: Which pollutant is secondary?
questions.explanation: Ground-level ozone (O₃) forms in the atmosphere → secondary.

questions.stem: Microbes found in water include all except:
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are molecules, not microbes.

questions.stem: What does NOT constitute an air pollution control measure?
questions.explanation: Tall chimneys disperse — they don't reduce emissions.

questions.stem: The main reason we do not have Nuclear Energy in Greece:
questions.explanation: Greece is highly seismic.

questions.stem: Regarding investigation of outbreaks, which is true?
questions.explanation: A central aim is identifying the causative agent.

questions.stem: Regarding common source epidemics, which is true?

options.D: Differs from point-source when incubation is long

questions.explanation: Common/point-source curves are log-normal → right-skewed.

questions.stem: Two toxins, each specific for diarrhea or vomiting, are produced by:
questions.explanation: Bacillus cereus — diarrhoeal + emetic toxins.

questions.stem: Regarding foodborne infections, all apply except:

options.D: Multiplies in the food in high concentration

questions.explanation: In-food multiplication = intoxication.

questions.stem: Which method is NOT a way to disinfect drinking water?
questions.explanation: Aluminium sulfate = coagulant, not disinfectant.

questions.stem: Regarding changes in weight:

options.D: Energy balance matters only with ideal macronutrients

questions.explanation: Weight = overall energy balance → the specific claims are all wrong.

questions.stem: The primary greenhouse gas is:
questions.explanation: CO₂.

questions.stem: Stratospheric ozone is destroyed by:
questions.explanation: CFCs.

questions.stem: Which is correct regarding ideal intake?
questions.explanation: Vitamins provide no calories; micronutrients are necessary → none correct.

questions.stem: Regarding the risk of chemicals in food, all are correct except:
questions.explanation: 'Aquaculture' is a farming practice, not a chemical hazard.

questions.stem: A protozoan disease that can be waterborne is all except:
questions.explanation: Onchocerciasis is a nematode spread by blackflies — not a waterborne protozoan.

questions.stem: Bottled water must contain zero cfu/mL of the following, except:
questions.explanation: Heterotrophic flora has an allowed limit.

questions.stem: Regarding micronutrients:

options.A: Not necessary for the whole population
options.B: Essential in small doses via supplements

questions.explanation: Needed by everyone, best from food → all wrong.

questions.stem: Drinking water is allowed to contain up to:
questions.explanation: 0 enterococci/100 mL.

questions.stem: Stratospheric ozone is destroyed by:
questions.explanation: CFCs.

questions.stem: Regarding BMI, which is true?
questions.explanation: Cut-offs not valid at all ages → none.

questions.stem: The most sensitive ecosystem on the planet is:
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean.

questions.stem: Which is true for low-risk areas for traveler's diarrhea?
questions.explanation: Low-risk = developed regions incl. N & Central Europe.

questions.stem: Respirable particles (PM10) do NOT cause:
questions.explanation: PM10 harms heart/lungs, not skin.

questions.stem: Regarding HEALTH EDUCATION methods, which is true?
questions.explanation: Tailor to the audience's characteristics & education level.

questions.stem: In foodborne infection, all true except:

options.D: Multiplies in the food to high concentration

questions.explanation: In-food multiplication = intoxication.

questions.stem: Which disease has increased risk in livestock farmers?
questions.explanation: Brucellosis.

questions.stem: What does the attack rate indicate in an outbreak?

options.A: Always the same for a communicable disease

questions.explanation: Attack rate measures how widespread the outbreak is.

questions.stem: The primary greenhouse gas is:
questions.explanation: CO₂.

questions.stem: Microbes found in water include all except:
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides aren't microbes.

questions.stem: Which foods are safe for preventing traveler's diarrhea?
questions.explanation: Hot, freshly-cooked food is safest.

questions.stem: Which pollutant is secondary?
questions.explanation: O₃ is secondary.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding BASIC METABOLISM?

options.B: Can be influenced both directions by medication
options.C: Slightly increases with age in adulthood

questions.explanation: Drugs can raise or lower BMR (it falls with age).

questions.stem: Emerging pathogenic microorganisms include all except:
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae is not emerging.

questions.stem: Which air pollution source is NOT artificial?
questions.explanation: Saharan dust is natural.

questions.stem: Which method is NOT a way to disinfect drinking water?
questions.explanation: Aluminium sulfate = coagulation.

questions.stem: Which are characteristics of traveler's diarrhea?

options.C: Never with fever or bloody stools

questions.explanation: Defined as ≥3 unformed stools/24 h.

questions.stem: Two toxins, each specific for diarrhea or vomiting, are produced by:
questions.explanation: Bacillus cereus.

questions.stem: In the hospital, all the following are reservoirs except:
questions.explanation: Visitors are the least-typical reservoir.

questions.stem: Asbestos harms health through:
questions.explanation: Inhaled fibres damage the respiratory tract.

questions.stem: The most frequent cause of foodborne epidemics is:
questions.explanation: Salmonella.

questions.stem: Regarding fluid intake, which is true?

options.A: BMI>30 drink less to avoid retention

questions.explanation: Needs rise with physical activity.

questions.stem: Food contamination occurs from all except:

options.B: Water used for cleaning processing areas

questions.explanation: Clean processing water isn't a source; rodents, sick animals, carriers are.

questions.stem: Which is NOT associated with occupational lung disease?
questions.explanation: Silica/asbestos/coal → specific pneumoconioses; generic ambient PM<10 μm is environmental.

questions.stem: Regarding investigation of outbreaks, which is true?
questions.explanation: A key aim is identifying the agent.

questions.stem: The Minamata disease (Japan 1953) was caused by:
questions.explanation: Mercury (methylmercury).

questions.stem: Which is NOT true regarding BMI?

options.C: A valid indicator of physical health

questions.explanation: BMI = weight/height² — does not fall proportionally with height.

questions.stem: An epidemic outbreak requires the following, except:

options.B: Vaccination of a large part of the population

questions.explanation: Vaccination prevents outbreaks — not a requirement.

questions.stem: Which natural resource is renewable?
questions.explanation: Fertile soil is renewable if managed.

questions.stem: In foodborne infection, all true except:

options.A: Multiplies in the food to high concentration

questions.explanation: In-food multiplication = intoxication.

questions.stem: Meta-analysis is:

options.D: Quantitative synthesis of results from individual studies

questions.explanation: Meta-analysis = quantitative pooling of study results.

questions.stem: Thermal inversion is:
questions.explanation: A meteorological phenomenon that then traps pollution.

questions.stem: Highest mortality (5–10%) for foodborne diseases:
questions.explanation: Botulism — high case-fatality (listeriosis also high).

questions.stem: Factors contributing to emergence of infectious diseases include:
questions.explanation: Fast international transport spreads emerging pathogens.

questions.stem: HUS (diarrhea, blood, acute renal failure) is caused by:
questions.explanation: E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga-toxin) → HUS.

questions.stem: In a double-blind RCT:

options.C: Neither the researcher nor the patient knows

questions.explanation: Neither party knows.

questions.stem: All true regarding bottled waters, except:

options.D: Plastic favors microbes more than glass

questions.explanation: There IS concern for Giardia & Cryptosporidium.

questions.stem: Solar radiation does not contain:
questions.explanation: Solar radiation = IR+visible+UV; radio waves is the 'not contained' option.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding calories consumed?
questions.explanation: Needs vary with physical activity.

questions.stem: What is NOT an air pollution control measure?
questions.explanation: Tall chimneys disperse, don't reduce.

questions.stem: Respirable particles (PM10) do NOT cause:
questions.explanation: Not skin.

questions.stem: Which energy is non-renewable?
questions.explanation: Nuclear.

questions.stem: Percentage of antibiotics worldwide that goes to animals:
questions.explanation: Roughly half (≈50%+).

questions.stem: Which disease has increased risk in livestock farmers?
questions.explanation: Brucellosis.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding BASIC METABOLISM?

options.D: Can be influenced both directions by medication

questions.explanation: Drugs shift BMR both ways (BMR falls with age).

questions.stem: Sulfates in water result in:
questions.explanation: High sulfate → laxative effect.

questions.stem: Of 100 wedding guests, 11 developed gastroenteritis within 24 h. Attack rate?
Which measure of association is estimated in a case-control study?
questions.explanation: Case-control → odds ratio.

questions.stem: All true regarding dioxins, except:

options.C: Trap in animal fat via water

questions.explanation: Dioxins are lipophilic/hydrophobic — statement reversed.

questions.stem: Hospital epidemics investigated for all except:

options.C: Allow it to progress until natural immunity

questions.explanation: Never 'natural immunity'.

questions.stem: Emerging pathogenic microorganisms include all except:
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae is not emerging.

questions.stem: Upper acceptable noise limit for safe work:
questions.explanation: WHO ≈ <75 dB for 8-h shift.

questions.stem: Which is NOT important for defining a case in an outbreak?
questions.explanation: Not family history.

questions.stem: Regarding the Mediterranean diet pyramid, which is true?
questions.explanation: Med diet = limited meat.

questions.stem: What is true about hepatitis A?

options.B: Most common preventable disease in travelers developed→developing

questions.explanation: Hep A = most common vaccine-preventable travel infection (a vaccine exists).

questions.stem: Aflatoxins can be found in:
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins → cereal grains & nuts.

questions.stem: Which has one of the highest mortality rates (5–10%)?
questions.explanation: Botulinum toxin — high case-fatality.

questions.stem: Cumulative incidence is used in which cohort, due to the absence of losses during follow-up?
questions.explanation: CI assumes complete follow-up (no losses) → a closed cohort.

questions.stem: After initial observations, what must follow for reliable conclusions?
questions.explanation: Descriptive observations → systematic epidemiological checks (analytic investigation).

questions.stem: Random-digit dialling identifies what type of controls?
questions.explanation: Sampling the general public → population controls.

questions.stem: A prerequisite for foodborne infection includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: In-food multiplication = intoxication.

questions.stem: The ___ Mortality Ratio quantifies increase/decrease vs a reference population:
questions.explanation: Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR).

questions.stem: Which is true regarding calorie intake?
questions.explanation: Depends heavily on physical activity.

questions.stem: Recommended daily carbohydrate intake?
questions.explanation: ≈ 130 g/day.

questions.stem: John Snow's cholera hypothesis was that it spreads:

options.A: Person to person via inhaled particles

questions.explanation: Snow proved spread via faecally-contaminated water.

questions.stem: When RR of two factors together is GREATER than the product of individual RRs:
questions.explanation: Exceeding the product = departure from multiplicativity.

questions.stem: A bidirectional cohort study:

options.C: Has both prospective and retrospective elements

questions.explanation: Ambidirectional = both components.

questions.stem: Which measure best describes women developing hypertension during 121,600 person-years?
questions.explanation: Person-years → incidence rate.

questions.stem: Bacterial indicators of water signify that:
questions.explanation: They indicate faecal contamination.

questions.stem: What ensures impartial assignment & balances known+unknown confounders (large n)?
questions.explanation: Randomization balances unknown confounders.

questions.stem: Which is true?

options.B: Public health is a branch of epidemiology
options.C: Both are branches of medical statistics
options.D: Epidemiology is a branch of public health

questions.explanation: Epidemiology is a branch of public health.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a component of clean, unpolluted air?
questions.explanation: Ammonia.

questions.stem: When the excess RR for two factors is LESS than the sum of individual RRs:
questions.explanation: Sub-additive = antagonism.

questions.stem: The 'water crisis' is likely because:
questions.explanation: Both disrupted cycle + rising consumption.

questions.stem: Regarding weight changes:

options.A: Energy balance only with ideal macronutrients

questions.explanation: Weight = energy balance → specific claims false.

questions.stem: The advantage of a matched case-control study is that:
questions.explanation: Matching → increases power/efficiency.

questions.stem: A new treatment prolongs survival. Effect on disease prevalence?
questions.explanation: Prevalence = incidence × duration → rises.

questions.stem: Which waist circumference is normal for a woman?
questions.explanation: Women normal <80 cm → 75 cm.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true about Burnout (ICD-11)?
questions.explanation: Burnout ≠ suicidal thoughts.

questions.stem: Which term = analyzing who/where/when patterns over time?
questions.explanation: Who/where/when = disease distribution.

questions.stem: Epidemiology (study of disease frequency) is NOT related to:
questions.explanation: All three ARE needed → all of the above.

questions.stem: The 10 principles — first rules on human experimentation — were formulated in:
questions.explanation: Nuremberg Code = 10 principles.

questions.stem: Which waters require purification before use?
questions.explanation: Both require treatment/disinfection.

questions.stem: The three components of the epidemiologic triangle:
questions.explanation: Host · Pathogen · Environment.

questions.stem: Who was the first epidemiologist/statistician/demographer (London death records)?
questions.explanation: John Graunt.

questions.stem: Enroll women with preterm birth vs normal delivery, then interview about smoking. Study type?
questions.explanation: Sampling on outcome → case-control.

questions.stem: Which study identifies cases + a sample of the source population without disease as controls?
questions.explanation: Cases + population controls = case-control.

questions.stem: Which represents a low to moderate level of effect modification?
questions.explanation: Deviation from additivity = lower-scale effect modification.

questions.stem: When age-specific mortality rates in the study population are known, the appropriate method is:
questions.explanation: Known study-pop rates → direct (indirect is for unknown).

questions.stem: Which inorganic element is NOT considered a trace element?
questions.explanation: Trace = iron. Sodium (like Cl, Mg, S) is a macromineral.

questions.stem: The three components of the epidemiological triangle:
questions.explanation: Host · Pathogen · Environment.

questions.stem: Epidemiology (study of disease frequency) is NOT related to:
questions.explanation: All ARE necessary → all describe necessary elements.

questions.stem: Which are the most widespread of all mycotoxins?
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins (A. flavus).

questions.stem: What is NOT true about disease determinants?

options.B: Exclusively factors that cause disease in some people

questions.explanation: Determinants can be protective too — not 'exclusively' causative.

questions.stem: The ability of a study to demonstrate an association if one truly exists is:
questions.explanation: Statistical power.

questions.stem: Regarding the Mediterranean diet pyramid, which is correct?
questions.explanation: Med diet = limited meat (~200–400 g/week).

questions.stem: In the work environment, what is true regarding noise?
questions.explanation: WHO ≈ 75 dB for 8-h exposure.

questions.stem: Two patients, same weight and height. Which is true?

options.B: No safe conclusions without body composition
options.D: Same BMI so same fat distribution

questions.explanation: BMI depends only on weight & height → same BMI & classification.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a component of clean, pollutant-free air?
questions.explanation: Ammonia.

questions.stem: The thalidomide disaster mainly highlighted:
questions.explanation: → need for rigorous drug-safety (teratogenicity) testing.

questions.stem: Regarding fluid intake needs, which is true?
questions.explanation: Needs vary with physical activity.

questions.stem: Two factors together cause disease MORE than expected:
questions.explanation: Supra-additive = synergy.

questions.stem: Bacterial dysentery (blood and mucus) is caused by:
questions.explanation: Shigella.

questions.stem: Random-digit dialling identifies what type of controls?
questions.explanation: Population controls.

questions.stem: When RR of two factors together is GREATER than the product of individual RRs:
questions.explanation: Beating the product = deviation from multiplicativity.

questions.stem: The main bacterial indicator is:
questions.explanation: E. coli.

questions.stem: Greatest disadvantage of using the general population as a comparison group:
questions.explanation: Workers healthier than the public → healthy worker effect.

questions.stem: The EU limits for the Air Quality Index:
questions.explanation: WHO stricter (health-based); EU less strict.

questions.stem: Conducted one of the first recorded clinical trials (17th c. onward):
questions.explanation: James Lind.

questions.stem: Standardization estimates the ___ role of certain variables:
questions.explanation: Removes the confounding role (esp. age).

questions.stem: Which study uses the population (not the individual) as the unit of analysis?
questions.explanation: Ecological studies.

questions.stem: Which is an example of incidence rate?

options.A: Men with high BP at annual check-up
options.C: New breast cancer cases during 200 person-years
options.D: Children with spina bifida per 1,000 births

questions.explanation: Person-years denominator = incidence rate.

questions.stem: Regarding management of food poisoning, all are true except:
questions.explanation: Oral rehydration IS given → this statement is false.

questions.stem: Who was the first epidemiologist/statistician/demographer?
questions.explanation: John Graunt.

questions.stem: A prerequisite for foodborne INTOXICATION is all except:
questions.explanation: Intoxication is toxin-driven — does not require intestinal multiplication.

questions.stem: How is climate change likely to affect malaria prevalence?
questions.explanation: Warming + rainfall shifts favour mosquito breeding.

questions.stem: Cumulative incidence (average risk) is the measure of choice in which cohort, because there are no losses during follow-up?
questions.explanation: Closed (fixed) cohort. Cumulative incidence assumes everyone is followed for the whole period with no loss to follow-up.

questions.stem: James Lind is best known for which contribution?

options.B: The first recorded controlled clinical trial (scurvy → citrus)

questions.explanation: The first recorded controlled clinical trial — HMS Salisbury, 1747, citrus fruit cures scurvy.

questions.stem: Bacterial indicators of water signify that:

options.B: The water has mixed with intestinal (faecal) contents
options.C: The water is safe to drink

questions.explanation: The water has mixed with intestinal contents — faecal contamination, correlated with pathogens.

questions.stem: An advantage of a matched case-control study is that:

options.D: It removes the need for controls

questions.explanation: Study power is increased — matching improves efficiency and controls confounding.

questions.stem: A new treatment prolongs survival but doesn't cure. Effect on disease prevalence?
questions.explanation: Prevalence increases. Prevalence = incidence × duration; longer survival lengthens duration.

questions.stem: The study of disease frequency is NOT related to which of the following?
questions.explanation: None — all of them are necessary (criteria, numerator of cases, denominator population).

questions.stem: A case-control study is characterised by:

options.A: Sampling by exposure and following forward
options.B: Sampling by disease status and looking back at exposure, using the odds ratio
options.C: Using the population as the unit of analysis

questions.explanation: Sampling by disease status, looking back at exposure; measure = odds ratio. Ideal for rare diseases.

questions.stem: Which best describes the ecological fallacy?

options.A: Confusing correlation with causation in one person
options.B: Inferring individual-level relationships from group-level data

questions.explanation: Inferring individual-level associations from group (population)-level data.

questions.stem: 'Equipoise' in a clinical trial means:

options.B: Genuine uncertainty about which treatment is better — required for an ethical RCT

questions.explanation: Genuine uncertainty about which treatment is superior. An RCT is only ethical when equipoise exists.

questions.stem: What is added in the FINAL step of drinking-water treatment?
questions.explanation: Chlorine (chlorination) — the final disinfection step, leaving a protective residual. Aluminium sulfate is an earlier coagulation step.

questions.stem: The thalidomide disaster mainly highlighted the need for:
questions.explanation: Careful drug safety assessment, especially teratogenicity testing in pregnancy.

questions.stem: The main bacterial indicator organism for water is:
questions.explanation: E. coli (faecal coliform) — the principal indicator of faecal contamination.

questions.stem: Compared with WHO guidelines, the EU air-quality limits are:
questions.explanation: Less strict. WHO guidelines are purely health-based; EU limits balance economics/feasibility, so they're more lenient.

questions.stem: Standardization is a method used to estimate the ___ role of certain variables so populations are comparable:
questions.explanation: Confounding. Standardization removes the confounding effect (usually of age).

questions.stem: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) formula is:
questions.explanation: SMR = observed ÷ expected deaths (×100). From indirect standardization.

questions.stem: Meta-analysis is best defined as:

options.B: The quantitative (statistical) synthesis of results from individual studies
options.C: Analysis of a single large trial

questions.explanation: The quantitative synthesis of results from individual studies — an optional statistical part of a systematic review.

questions.stem: Thermal inversion is:

options.B: A meteorological phenomenon that traps pollutants near the ground

questions.explanation: A meteorological phenomenon — a warm air layer traps cooler air below, so pollutants accumulate.

questions.stem: Which statement about RR and OR is correct?

options.B: Both equal 1 under the null; OR is the measure of case-control studies
options.C: OR is used in cohort studies only

questions.explanation: RR = OR = 1 under the null (no association); the OR (cross-product ratio) is the measure of case-control studies.

questions.stem: 'Genomic surveillance' refers to:

options.A: Sequencing pathogen genomes to track spread and variants

questions.explanation: Sequencing pathogen genomes to monitor transmission, evolution and emerging variants.

questions.stem: Asbestos primarily harms health through:
questions.explanation: The respiratory tract — inhaled fibres cause asbestosis, lung cancer, mesothelioma.

questions.stem: If a relative comparison measure equals 0.81 (less than one), the exposure is:
questions.explanation: Protective. An RR/OR below 1 = lower disease risk (prevents disease).

questions.stem: The Tuskegee Syphilis Study is remembered as research that was:

options.B: Legal at the time but deeply unethical (no informed consent)

questions.explanation: Legal at the time but unethical — men weren't told their diagnosis or that treatment was withheld; drove modern consent/IRB rules.

questions.stem: 'Excess mortality' (used for Storm Daniel, Thessaly 2023) is defined as:
questions.explanation: Observed − expected deaths — the extra deaths beyond what would normally be expected.

questions.stem: Of 1,000 newborns, 100 had serious birth defects and 20 of those died in year 1; 90 of the 900 without defects also died. Cumulative incidence of mortality in the whole population?
True or False: 'Occupational risk is anything that has the potential to cause harm.'

options.B: False — that defines a HAZARD, not risk

questions.explanation: False. That defines a hazard. Occupational risk = the likelihood AND severity of injury/disease from exposure to a hazard.

questions.stem: If there is NO association, the values of (risk ratio, odds ratio, risk difference) are:
questions.explanation: 1, 1, 0. Ratios (RR, OR) = 1 under the null; the risk difference (absolute) = 0.

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the Bradford Hill criteria for causation?
questions.explanation: Complexity. The nine Hill criteria: strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, biological gradient, plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy.

questions.stem: The 1993 Milwaukee waterborne outbreak was caused by:
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium, a protozoan parasite (not a bacterium!). ~400,000 affected — largest US waterborne outbreak, following heavy rainfall.

questions.stem: In plants, increased atmospheric CO₂ tends to:
questions.explanation: Increase carbohydrate content while diluting protein and micronutrients.

questions.stem: Which CANNOT be caused by waterborne infection?
questions.explanation: Myocardial infarction. Diarrhoea, cholera and typhoid are waterborne; a heart attack is not an infectious waterborne disease.

questions.stem: Who developed the concept of the life table by analysing London death records?
questions.explanation: John Graunt — father of demography; first epidemiologist/statistician/demographer.

questions.stem: An ambidirectional (bidirectional) study is a type of:

options.B: Cohort study with both prospective and retrospective elements

questions.explanation: A cohort study with both prospective and retrospective components.

questions.stem: Malnutrition differs from undernutrition in that malnutrition:

options.B: Encompasses ALL forms — under-, over-nutrition and micronutrient imbalance

questions.explanation: Malnutrition is the umbrella term (under-, over-nutrition, micronutrient deficiency); undernutrition is specifically deficiency.

questions.stem: The odds ratio is the characteristic measure of which design?
questions.explanation: Case-control study.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a type of environmental pollution?
questions.explanation: Technology. Recognised types: water, air, soil, thermal, noise, light.

questions.stem: Epidemiology is best described as:
questions.explanation: A branch of public health — one of its basic sciences.

questions.stem: The Belmont Report's three core principles are:
questions.explanation: Respect for autonomy, beneficence, justice. (Distinct from Nuremberg's 10 principles.)

questions.stem: A cross-sectional study is best described as:

options.A: A 'snapshot' measuring exposure and outcome at a single point in time

questions.explanation: A snapshot at one point in time — good for prevalence.

sections.title: 1 · What Epidemiology Is + Key History
sections.html:
Epidemiology = study of the distribution & determinants of disease frequency in human populations, and applying this to control health problems.

Epidemiology is a branch of PUBLIC HEALTH (not vice-versa) — one of its basic sciences.

  • Distribution = who, where, when (person, place, time).
  • Determinants = exposures/risk factors; modifiable or not, and can be protective OR causative (not exclusively harmful).
  • Epidemiologic triangle: Host Pathogen (agent) Environment

The 3 founding figures

FigureCenturyClaim to fame
John Graunt17thFather of demography. Analysed London parish death records (from ~1532), built the life table. "First epidemiologist, statistician & demographer."
James Lind18thRoyal-Navy physician. First recorded controlled clinical trial (HMS Salisbury, 1747) — citrus cures scurvy.
John Snow19thShowed cholera spreads via faecally-contaminated drinking water (Broad St pump). Killed the "miasma" idea.
Trap: "first clinical trial" = Lind; "first epidemiologist / death records / life table" = Graunt. Don't swap.

sections.title: 2 · Measures of Disease Frequency
sections.html:
Populations

  • Fixed / closed cohort: permanent membership, defined by an event (born 1982, study enrollees). No new entries.
  • Dynamic / open cohort: transient membership by a state (residents of a city).

Prevalence vs Incidence

PrevalenceIncidence
CountsExisting casesNew cases
DenominatorTotal populationPopulation at risk only
UseBurden, planning, resourcesAetiologic research, evaluating prevention

Two flavours of incidence

  • Cumulative incidence (CI) = risk. New cases ÷ pop. at risk at start; time in words. Assumes NO loss to follow-up → best in a closed/fixed cohort.
  • Incidence rate (IR) = speed. New cases ÷ person-time; time is intrinsic to the denominator. Handles losses → clue "person-years" = incidence rate.

Special "rates"

  • Attack rate = new cases ÷ pop. at risk in an outbreak → quantifies the extent (11 of 100 ill = 11%).
  • Case-fatality rate = deaths from X ÷ people with X.
Prevalence = Incidence × Duration. A treatment that prolongs survival (↑duration) → prevalence RISES.

sections.title: 3 · Measures of Association (RR / OR / RD)
sections.html:
MeasureTypeNullProtectiveHarmful
Risk Difference (RD)Absolute0< 0> 0
Risk/Rate Ratio (RR)Relative1< 1> 1
Odds Ratio (OR)Relative (ad/bc)1< 1> 1
  • No association ⇒ RR = 1, OR = 1, RD = 0 → the "1, 1, 0" answer.
  • A relative measure of 0.81 (<1) ⇒ protective / prevents disease.
  • "Relative risk" = umbrella for all relative measures; usually the risk ratio.
  • OR is the measure of a case-control study.

sections.title: 4 · Study Designs
sections.html:
• Descriptive = describes disease by person, place, time (case report/series, cross-sectional, ecological). Generates hypotheses.
  • Analytic = tests hypotheses (case-control, cohort, experimental).
DesignLogicBest forMeasure
Cross-sectional"Snapshot" at one timePrevalencePrevalence ratio
EcologicalPopulation is unit of analysisHypothesis generation—
Case-controlSample by disease, look back at exposureRare diseases, long latencyOdds ratio
CohortSample by exposure, follow forwardRare exposures, single agentRR / IRR

Cohort timing

  • Prospective: exposure now → future outcome.
  • Retrospective: both already happened (records).
  • Ambidirectional / bidirectional: both prospective and retrospective elements.

Case-control specifics

  • Control types: population (random-digit dialling, voter/tax lists), hospital/clinical, friend/relative/spouse, deceased.
  • Hospital controls should share the cases' catchment area.
  • Matching controls confounding & increases power/efficiency. Ratio up to 4:1 boosts power.
"Enroll women with preterm birth vs normal delivery, then ask about smoking" = sampling on outcome → case-control.

sections.title: 5 · Experimental Studies / RCTs + Ethics
sections.html:
• Experiments are always prospective; not all are randomised.
  • Double-blind: neither researcher nor patient knows the allocation.
  • Randomization: balances known AND unknown confounders (large n).
  • Equipoise: genuine uncertainty which treatment is better — RCT only ethical when equipoise exists.
  • IRB / ethics committee approves the protocol (consent, recruitment) before starting.
Doc / eventKey point
Nuremberg CodePost-Nazi trials. The 10 principles; emphasised voluntary informed consent.
Declaration of HelsinkiWith Nuremberg, basis of human-subjects ethics.
TuskegeeUS PHS, 1932. Legal but unethical — no consent, treatment withheld.
ThalidomideTeratogenic → need for careful drug-safety assessment.
BelmontRespect for autonomy, beneficence, justice.

sections.title: 6 · Confounding, Effect Modification & Causality
sections.html:
Confounding — the "3rd variable" problem

Distorts (mixes) the exposure–disease link. Must be (1) associated with exposure, (2) an independent risk factor for disease, (3) NOT on the causal pathway. Shown with a DAG.

Control by: restriction, randomization, matching, stratified & multivariable analysis.

Effect modification / interaction

  • Synergy = combined effect more than expected.
  • Antagonism = combined excess RR less than the sum of individual RRs.
  • Combined RR > product of individual RRs → departure from multiplicativity.
  • Deviation from additivity = lower/moderate effect modification.

Bradford Hill criteria (9)

Strength · Consistency · Specificity · Temporality · Biological gradient · Plausibility · Coherence · Experiment · Analogy.

Trap: "Complexity" is NOT a Hill criterion. If a stem lists Association/Specificity/Plausibility/Complexity, the odd one is Complexity (real one = Consistency).

Ecological fallacy = inferring individual-level relationships from group-level data.

sections.title: 7 · Standardization, SMR & Meta-analysis
sections.html:
Standardization removes the confounding role of variables (esp. age) so populations can be compared. Crude rates can't be compared directly (age structures differ).

DirectIndirect (→ SMR)
UsesAge-specific rates of the study pop. + standard pop.Age-specific rates of the standard pop. + age distribution of study pop.
WhenStudy-pop. rates are KNOWNStudy-pop. rates unknown/unstable
  • SMR = observed ÷ expected deaths (×100).
  • Excess mortality = observed − expected deaths (e.g. Storm Daniel, Thessaly 2023).
  • Meta-analysis = the QUANTITATIVE (statistical) synthesis of individual studies (optional part of a systematic review).
  • Statistical power = ability to detect an association if one truly exists.

sections.title: 8 · Hygiene of Water
sections.html:
Sources

  • Groundwater / aquifer: soil-filtered, cleanest.
  • Surface water: unsafe raw, needs treatment. Both types generally need purification/disinfection.

Indicator organisms

  • Main bacterial indicator = E. coli (faecal coliform).
  • Indicators signify faecal contamination — water mixed with intestinal contents.
  • Drinking water: 0 enterococci / 100 mL.
  • Bottled water: 0 cfu/mL of E. coli, enterococci, Pseudomonas — heterotrophic flora has an allowed limit (the "except").

Disinfection / treatment

  • Methods: chlorination, ozonation, UV.
  • Aluminium sulfate = coagulation, NOT disinfection.
  • Chlorine is the FINAL step (residual disinfection).

Waterborne disease

  • Bacteria: cholera, typhoid, E. coli O157:H7, Salmonella, Campylobacter.
  • Protozoa: Cryptosporidium, Giardia, Entamoeba (amoebic dysentery).
  • Sulfates → laxative effect.
Milwaukee 1993: biggest US waterborne outbreak — Cryptosporidium (a protozoan, NOT bacteria). Follows heavy rain, like Storm Daniel 2023.

sections.title: 9 · The Air We Breathe / Pollution
sections.html:
Clean air

N₂ ≈ 78% · O₂ ≈ 21% · Ar ≈ 0.93% · CO₂ < 0.04% + trace gases.

Ammonia is NOT a component of clean air. (Repeatedly tested.)

Pollutants

  • Primary = emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, hydrocarbons, PM).
  • Secondary = formed by reactions → ground-level ozone (O₃).
  • Thermal inversion = a meteorological phenomenon that traps pollutants (Great London Smog 1952).
  • PM10 → cardiovascular/respiratory harm + premature mortality — NOT skin.

Greenhouse / ozone

  • Primary greenhouse gas = CO₂.
  • Stratospheric ozone destroyed by CFCs.

AQI & standards

  • AQI: 0–500, higher = worse; set by the "dominant pollutant".
  • WHO stricter (health-based only); EU limits less strict (balance economics/feasibility).

Control & energy

  • Real controls: cleaner fuels, renewables, tree cover. Tall chimneys are NOT a real control (just disperse).
  • Least polluting fuel = natural gas; non-renewable = nuclear; renewable = wind/solar/hydro/fertile soil.
  • No nuclear in Greece → earthquake-prone.

sections.title: 10 · Food & Nutrition
sections.html:
Foodborne INFECTION vs INTOXICATION

InfectionIntoxication
CauseLive microbe ingestedPre-formed toxin in food
Multiply in food?No (just present)Yes
Multiply in gut?YesNo
Infection "except" = "must multiply in the food" (that's intoxication). Intoxication "except" = "multiplies in the intestinal tract" (that's infection).

Key organisms

  • Highest mortality (5–10%): botulism (C. botulinum) & listeriosis.
  • Bacillus cereus = two toxins (diarrhoeal + emetic).
  • Most foodborne epidemics = Salmonella; most common EU zoonosis = Campylobacter.
  • Bacterial dysentery = Shigella; HUS (bloody diarrhoea + AKI) = E. coli O157:H7.

Toxins

  • Aflatoxins (Aspergillus flavus) = most widespread mycotoxins; in cereal grains & nuts.
  • Dioxins = lipophilic/hydrophobic, accumulate in fat, carcinogenic.

Nutrition

  • BMI: 18.5–25 normal but not at all ages → "none correct". Same weight+height = same BMI & classification (ignores body composition).
  • Calorie needs depend on physical activity; BMR shifted both ways by medication.
  • Recommended carbohydrate ≈ 130 g/day; Mediterranean diet meat ≈ 200–400 g/week.
  • Trace elements = iron/zinc; Na, Cl, Mg, S, Ca, K are macrominerals.
  • Weight = energy balance, not one macronutrient.

Traveller's diarrhoea

  • ≥ 3 unformed stools/24 h; low-risk = developed regions.
  • Safe = very hot, freshly cooked; Hep A = most common vaccine-preventable travel infection.

sections.title: 11 · Occupational & Environmental Hygiene
sections.html:
• Hazard = anything with potential to cause harm.
  • Occupational risk = likelihood AND severity of injury/disease from a hazard.
T/F trap: "Occupational risk is anything with potential to cause harm" → FALSE. That's a hazard.
  • Noise: WHO safe < 75 dB for an 8-h shift.
  • Asbestos harms via respiratory tract → asbestosis, mesothelioma.
  • Occupational lung agents: silica, asbestos, coal.
  • Livestock farmers → brucellosis (and TB).
  • Healthy worker effect: workers healthier than general public → main disadvantage of using the general population as comparison.

Burnout (ICD-11)

From chronic unmanaged workplace stress: exhaustion, cynicism, reduced efficacy. NOT suicidal thoughts (the "not true" option).

Pollution types

Water · Air · Soil · Thermal · Noise · Light. "Technology" is NOT a pollution type. Minamata 1953 = mercury; most sensitive ecosystem = Mediterranean Sea.

sections.title: 12 · Outbreak Investigation & Emerging Diseases
sections.html:
Outbreak — 3 necessary conditions

  • A pathogenic agent infecting many.
  • An appropriate mode of transmission.
  • A sufficiently LARGE susceptible pool (NOT "limited"; vaccination is not required — it prevents outbreaks).

Investigation aims

  • Identify agent, source/reservoir, transmission, then intervene.
  • Never "let it evolve to natural immunity".
  • Case definition = symptoms, incubation, lab findings — NOT family history.
  • Hospital reservoirs: environment, colonised staff, colonised/infected patients (visitors least).

Emerging pathogens

  • Examples: protozoa (Cryptosporidium), H. pylori, Campylobacter. S. pneumoniae is NOT emerging.
  • Drivers: international transport, weak health systems, climate change, human–animal contact.

Climate change

  • Warming favours Cryptosporidium/Cyclospora; floods precede outbreaks.
  • Malaria: shifts temperature & rainfall favouring mosquito breeding.
  • ↑CO₂ in plants → ↑carbohydrate, ↓protein/micronutrients.

Health education

Approach depends on the audience's characteristics & education level; small groups best.
